 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Lovett_Hall.jpg/250px-Lovett_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lovett_Hall.jpg)

[Rice University](/wiki/Rice_University "Rice University")

The state's two most widely recognized flagship universities are [The University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/The_University_of_Texas_at_Austin "The University of Texas at Austin") and [Texas A&M University](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_University "Texas A&M University"), ranked as the 21st[[379]](#cite_note-389) and 41st[[380]](#cite_note-390) best universities in the nation according to 2020's latest Center for World University Rankings report, respectively. Some observers[[381]](#cite_note-391) also include the [University of Houston](/wiki/University_of_Houston "University of Houston") and [Texas Tech University](/wiki/Texas_Tech_University "Texas Tech University") as tier one flagships alongside UT Austin and A&M.[[382]](#cite_note-392)[[383]](#cite_note-393) The [Texas Higher Education Coordinating Board](/wiki/Texas_Higher_Education_Coordinating_Board "Texas Higher Education Coordinating Board") ranks the state's public universities into three distinct tiers:[[384]](#cite_note-UT_System_Tier_One-394)

* National Research Universities (Tier 1)[[385]](#cite_note-395)[[386]](#cite_note-396)
  + [The University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/The_University_of_Texas_at_Austin "The University of Texas at Austin")
  + [Texas A&M University](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_University "Texas A&M University")
  + [Texas Tech University](/wiki/Texas_Tech_University "Texas Tech University")
  + [University of Houston](/wiki/University_of_Houston "University of Houston")
  + [Rice University](/wiki/Rice_University "Rice University")
  + [The University of Texas Health Science Center at Houston](/wiki/The_University_of_Texas_Health_Science_Center_at_Houston "The University of Texas Health Science Center at Houston")
  + [The University of Texas at Arlington](/wiki/The_University_of_Texas_at_Arlington "The University of Texas at Arlington")
  + [The University of Texas at Dallas](/wiki/The_University_of_Texas_at_Dallas "The University of Texas at Dallas")
  + [The University of North Texas](/wiki/The_University_of_North_Texas "The University of North Texas")
  + [The University of Texas at El Paso](/wiki/The_University_of_Texas_at_El_Paso "The University of Texas at El Paso")
* Emerging Research Universities (Tier 2)[[384]](#cite_note-UT_System_Tier_One-394)
  + [The University of Texas at San Antonio](/wiki/The_University_of_Texas_at_San_Antonio "The University of Texas at San Antonio")
  + [Texas State University](/wiki/Texas_State_University "Texas State University")
* Comprehensive Universities (Tier 3)[[384]](#cite_note-UT_System_Tier_One-394)
  + All other public universities (25 in total)

The Carnegie Foundation classifies six of Texas's universities as Tier One research institutions:[[387]](#cite_note-397)

* [The University of Texas Health Science Center at Houston](/wiki/The_University_of_Texas_Health_Science_Center_at_Houston "The University of Texas Health Science Center at Houston")
* [Rice University](/wiki/Rice_University "Rice University")
* [The University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin")
* [Texas A&M University](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_University "Texas A&M University")
* [University of Houston](/wiki/University_of_Houston "University of Houston")
* [Texas Tech University](/wiki/Texas_Tech_University "Texas Tech University")

Texas's alternative affirmative action plan, [Texas House Bill 588](/wiki/Texas_House_Bill_588 "Texas House Bill 588"), guarantees Texas students who graduated in the top 10 percent of their high school class automatic admission to state-funded universities. This does not apply to [The University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/The_University_of_Texas_at_Austin "The University of Texas at Austin"), which automatically admits Texas students who graduated in the top 6 percent of their high school class.[[388]](#cite_note-398) The bill encourages demographic diversity while attempting to avoid problems stemming from the *[Hopwood v. Texas](/wiki/Hopwood_v._Texas "Hopwood v. Texas")* (1996) case.[[389]](#cite_note-399)

Thirty-six public universities exist in Texas, of which 32 belong to one of the six state university systems.[[390]](#cite_note-400)[[391]](#cite_note-401) Discovery of minerals on [Permanent University Fund](/wiki/Permanent_University_Fund "Permanent University Fund") land, particularly oil, has helped fund the rapid growth of the state's two largest university systems: the [University of Texas System](/wiki/University_of_Texas_System "University of Texas System") and the [Texas A&M System](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_University_System "Texas A&M University System"). The four other university systems: the [University of Houston System](/wiki/University_of_Houston_System "University of Houston System"), the [University of North Texas System](/wiki/University_of_North_Texas_System "University of North Texas System"), the [Texas State System](/wiki/Texas_State_University_System "Texas State University System"), and the [Texas Tech System](/wiki/Texas_Tech_University_System "Texas Tech University System") are not funded by the Permanent University Fund.[[392]](#cite_note-Smyrl-2020-402)

The University of Texas at Austin and Texas A&M University are the flagship universities of the [University of Texas System](/wiki/University_of_Texas_System "University of Texas System") and [Texas A&M University System](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_University_System "Texas A&M University System"), respectively. Both were established by the Texas Constitution and hold stakes in the Permanent University Fund.[[392]](#cite_note-Smyrl-2020-402)

The state has sought to expand the number of flagship universities by elevating some of its seven institutions designated as "emerging research universities". The two expected to emerge first are the University of Houston and Texas Tech University, likely in that order according to discussions on the House floor of the 82nd Texas Legislature.[[393]](#cite_note-403)

The state is home to various private institutions of higher learning—ranging from liberal arts colleges to a nationally recognized top-tier research university. [Rice University](/wiki/Rice_University "Rice University") in Houston is one of the leading teaching and research universities of the United States and is ranked the nation's 17th-best overall university by *U.S. News & World Report*.[[394]](#cite_note-404)

[Trinity University](/wiki/Trinity_University_(Texas) "Trinity University (Texas)"), a private, primarily undergraduate liberal arts university in San Antonio, has ranked first among universities granting primarily bachelor's and select master's degrees in the Western United States for 20 consecutive years by *U.S. News*.[[395]](#cite_note-405) Private universities include [Abilene Christian University](/wiki/Abilene_Christian_University "Abilene Christian University"), [Austin College](/wiki/Austin_College "Austin College"), [Baylor University](/wiki/Baylor_University "Baylor University"), [University of Mary Hardin–Baylor](/wiki/University_of_Mary_Hardin%E2%80%93Baylor "University of Mary Hardin–Baylor"), and [Southwestern University](/wiki/Southwestern_University "Southwestern University").[[396]](#cite_note-406)[[397]](#cite_note-407)[[398]](#cite_note-408)

Universities in Texas host three presidential libraries: [George Bush Presidential Library](/wiki/George_Bush_Presidential_Library "George Bush Presidential Library") at Texas A&M University,[[399]](#cite_note-409) the [Lyndon Baines Johnson Library and Museum](/wiki/Lyndon_Baines_Johnson_Library_and_Museum "Lyndon Baines Johnson Library and Museum") at The University of Texas at Austin,[[400]](#cite_note-410)[[401]](#cite_note-411) and the [George W. Bush Presidential Library](/wiki/George_W._Bush_Presidential_Library "George W. Bush Presidential Library") at [Southern Methodist University](/wiki/Southern_Methodist_University "Southern Methodist University").[[402]](#cite_note-412)

## Healthcare

Main article: [Healthcare in Texas](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Texas "Healthcare in Texas")

See also: [List of hospitals in Texas](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_Texas "List of hospitals in Texas")

Notwithstanding the concentration of elite medical centers in the state, [The Commonwealth Fund](/wiki/The_Commonwealth_Fund "The Commonwealth Fund") ranks the Texas healthcare system the third worst in the nation.[[403]](#cite_note-insurancenet-413) Texas ranks close to last in access to healthcare, quality of care, avoidable hospital spending, and equity.[[403]](#cite_note-insurancenet-413) In May 2006, Texas initiated the program "code red" in response to the report the state had 25.1 percent of the population without health insurance, the largest proportion in the nation.[[404]](#cite_note-414)

The [Trust for America's Health](/wiki/Trust_for_America%27s_Health "Trust for America's Health") ranked Texas 15th highest in adult [obesity](/wiki/Obesity "Obesity"): 27.2 percent of the state's population is obese.[[405]](#cite_note-415) The 2008 [Men's Health](/wiki/Men%27s_Health_(magazine) "Men's Health (magazine)") obesity survey ranked four Texas cities among the top 25 fattest cities in America: Houston ranked 6th, Dallas 7th, El Paso 8th, and [Arlington](/wiki/Arlington,_Texas "Arlington, Texas") 14th.[[406]](#cite_note-obese2-416) Texas had only one city (Austin, ranked 21st) in the top 25 "fittest cities" in America.[[406]](#cite_note-obese2-416) The state is ranked forty-second in the percentage of residents who engage in regular exercise according to a 2007 study.[[407]](#cite_note-417)

Texas has the [highest maternal mortality rate](/wiki/Maternal_Healthcare_System_in_Texas "Maternal Healthcare System in Texas") in the developed world, and the rate by which Texas women died from pregnancy-related complications doubled from 2010 to 2014, to 23.8 per 100,000—a rate unmatched in any other U.S. state or economically developed country.[[408]](#cite_note-418) In May 2021, the state legislature passed the [Texas Heartbeat Act](/wiki/Texas_Heartbeat_Act "Texas Heartbeat Act"), which banned [abortion](/wiki/Abortion "Abortion") from as early as six weeks of pregnancy, except to save the life of the mother. The Act allows private citizens to sue abortion providers and anyone else who assists in an abortion, except for the woman on whom the abortion is performed.[[409]](#cite_note-419)[[410]](#cite_note-420) On August 25, 2022, another law took effect that made committing abortion at any stage of pregnancy a felony punishable by life in prison.[[411]](#cite_note-421)

Access to allergy and immunology specialists in Texas is unevenly distributed. While the state is served by approximately 462 allergists and immunologists, these specialists are primarily concentrated in major urban centers such as Dallas, Houston, and Austin. In rural areas of Texas, the average availability is significantly lower, with approximately one specialist for every 50,000 residents.
[[412]](#cite_note-422)

### Medical research

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Texas_medical_center_sundown.jpg/250px-Texas_medical_center_sundown.jpg)](/wiki/File:Texas_medical_center_sundown.jpg)

The [Texas Medical Center](/wiki/Texas_Medical_Center "Texas Medical Center") in Houston

Texas has many elite research medical centers. The state has 15 [medical schools](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_Texas#Health_science "List of colleges and universities in Texas"),[[413]](#cite_note-423) four dental schools,[[414]](#cite_note-424) and two [optometry](/wiki/Optometry "Optometry") schools.[[415]](#cite_note-425) Texas has two [Biosafety Level 4](/wiki/Biosafety_Level_4 "Biosafety Level 4") (BSL-4) laboratories: one at [The University of Texas Medical Branch](/wiki/The_University_of_Texas_Medical_Branch "The University of Texas Medical Branch") (UTMB) in Galveston,[[416]](#cite_note-426) and the other at the [Southwest Foundation for Biomedical Research](/wiki/Southwest_Foundation_for_Biomedical_Research "Southwest Foundation for Biomedical Research") in San Antonio—the first privately owned BSL-4 lab in the United States.[[417]](#cite_note-427)

The [Texas Medical Center](/wiki/Texas_Medical_Center "Texas Medical Center") in Houston holds the world's largest concentration of research and healthcare institutions, with over 50 member institutions.[[418]](#cite_note-428) Texas Medical Center performs the most heart transplants in the world.[[419]](#cite_note-429) The [University of Texas M. D. Anderson Cancer Center](/wiki/University_of_Texas_M._D._Anderson_Cancer_Center "University of Texas M. D. Anderson Cancer Center") in Houston is a highly regarded academic institution that centers around cancer patient care, research, education and prevention.[[420]](#cite_note-430)

San Antonio's [South Texas Medical Center](/wiki/South_Texas_Medical_Center "South Texas Medical Center") facilities rank sixth in clinical medicine research impact in the United States.[[421]](#cite_note-431) The [University of Texas Health Science Center](/wiki/UTHSCSA "UTHSCSA") is another highly ranked research and educational institution in San Antonio.[[422]](#cite_note-432)[[423]](#cite_note-433)

Both the [American Heart Association](/wiki/American_Heart_Association "American Heart Association") and the [University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center](/wiki/University_of_Texas_Southwestern_Medical_Center "University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center") call Dallas home.[[424]](#cite_note-UTSWAboutUs-434) The [institution's medical school](/wiki/University_of_Texas_Southwestern_Medical_School "University of Texas Southwestern Medical School") employs the most medical school [Nobel laureates](/wiki/Nobel_laureates "Nobel laureates") in the world.[[424]](#cite_note-UTSWAboutUs-434)[[425]](#cite_note-435)

## Government and politics

The current [Texas Constitution](/wiki/Texas_Constitution "Texas Constitution") was adopted in 1876. Like many [states](/wiki/State_constitution_(United_States) "State constitution (United States)"), it explicitly provides for a separation of powers. The state's Bill of Rights is much larger than its [federal counterpart](/wiki/United_States_Bill_of_Rights "United States Bill of Rights"), and has provisions unique to Texas.[[426]](#cite_note-436)

### State government

Main article: [Government of Texas](/wiki/Government_of_Texas "Government of Texas")

Further information: [Law of Texas](/wiki/Law_of_Texas "Law of Texas")

See also: [List of Texas state agencies](/wiki/List_of_Texas_state_agencies "List of Texas state agencies")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Texas_State_Capitol_Night.jpg/330px-Texas_State_Capitol_Night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Texas_State_Capitol_Night.jpg)

The [Texas State Capitol](/wiki/Texas_State_Capitol "Texas State Capitol") at night

Texas has a plural [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_branch "Executive branch") system limiting the power of the governor, which is a weak executive compared to some other states. Except for the [secretary of state](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_of_Texas "Secretary of State of Texas"), voters elect executive officers independently; candidates are directly answerable to the public, not the governor.[[427]](#cite_note-pluralexec-437) This election system has led to some executive branches split between parties and reduced the ability of the governor to carry out a program. When [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") president [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") served as Texas's governor, the state had a [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") lieutenant governor, [Bob Bullock](/wiki/Bob_Bullock "Bob Bullock"). The executive branch positions consist of the [governor](/wiki/List_of_Governors_of_Texas "List of Governors of Texas"), [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Texas "Lieutenant Governor of Texas"), comptroller of public accounts, land commissioner, [attorney general](/wiki/Texas_Attorney_General "Texas Attorney General"), agriculture commissioner, the three-member [Texas Railroad Commission](/wiki/Texas_Railroad_Commission "Texas Railroad Commission"), the State Board of Education, and the secretary of state.[[427]](#cite_note-pluralexec-437)

The [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") [Texas Legislature](/wiki/Texas_Legislature "Texas Legislature") consists of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/Texas_House_of_Representatives "Texas House of Representatives"), with 150 members, and a [Senate](/wiki/Texas_Senate "Texas Senate"), with 31 members. The [Speaker of the House](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_Texas_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the Texas House of Representatives") leads the House, and the lieutenant governor, the Senate.[[428]](#cite_note-438) The Legislature meets in regular session biennially for just over a hundred days, but the governor can call for special sessions as often as desired (notably, the Legislature cannot call itself into session).[[429]](#cite_note-439) The state's [fiscal year](/wiki/Fiscal_year "Fiscal year") begins September 1.[[430]](#cite_note-440)

The [judiciary of Texas](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Texas "Judiciary of Texas") is among the most complex in the United States, with many layers and overlapping jurisdictions. Texas has two courts of last resort: the [Texas Supreme Court](/wiki/Texas_Supreme_Court "Texas Supreme Court"), for civil cases, and the [Texas Court of Criminal Appeals](/wiki/Texas_Court_of_Criminal_Appeals "Texas Court of Criminal Appeals"). Except for some municipal benches, partisan elections select judges at all levels of the judiciary; the governor fills vacancies by appointment.[[431]](#cite_note-441) Texas is notable for [its use of capital punishment](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_Texas "Capital punishment in Texas"), having led the country in executions since capital punishment was reinstated in the *[Gregg v. Georgia](/wiki/Gregg_v._Georgia "Gregg v. Georgia")* case.[[432]](#cite_note-442)

The [Texas Ranger Division](/wiki/Texas_Ranger_Division "Texas Ranger Division") of the [Texas Department of Public Safety](/wiki/Texas_Department_of_Public_Safety "Texas Department of Public Safety") is a [law enforcement agency](/wiki/Police "Police") with statewide [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction"). Over the years, the Texas Rangers have investigated crimes ranging from [murder](/wiki/Murder_in_Texas_law "Murder in Texas law") to political corruption. They have acted as [riot police](/wiki/Riot_police "Riot police") and as detectives, protected the Texas governor, tracked down fugitives, and functioned as a paramilitary force. The Texas Rangers were unofficially created by [Stephen F. Austin](/wiki/Stephen_F._Austin "Stephen F. Austin") in 1823 and formally constituted in 1835. The Rangers were integral to several important events of Texas history and some of the best-known criminal cases in the history of the [Old West](/wiki/American_Old_West "American Old West").[[433]](#cite_note-443)

The Texas constitution defines the responsibilities of county governments, which serve as agents of the state. Commissioners court and court judges are elected to serve as the administrative arm. Most cities in the state, those over 5,000 in population, have [home-rule](/wiki/Home-rule "Home-rule") governments. The vast majority of these have charters for [council-manager](/wiki/Council-manager "Council-manager") forms of government, by which voters elect council members, who hire a professional city manager as an operating officer.[[434]](#cite_note-444)

### Politics

Main article: [Politics of Texas](/wiki/Politics_of_Texas "Politics of Texas")

Further information: [Political party strength in Texas](/wiki/Political_party_strength_in_Texas "Political party strength in Texas")

See also: [Republican Party of Texas](/wiki/Republican_Party_of_Texas "Republican Party of Texas") and [Texas Democratic Party](/wiki/Texas_Democratic_Party "Texas Democratic Party")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/37_Lyndon_Johnson_3x4.jpg/250px-37_Lyndon_Johnson_3x4.jpg)](/wiki/File:37_Lyndon_Johnson_3x4.jpg)

[Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") of Texas, 36th president of the United States

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/George-W-Bush.jpeg/250px-George-W-Bush.jpeg)](/wiki/File:George-W-Bush.jpeg)

[George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") of Texas, 43rd president of the United States

The Democratic Party dominated [Texas politics](/wiki/Politics_of_Texas "Politics of Texas") from the turn of the 20th century, imposing racial segregation and white supremacy. It held power until after passage in the mid-1960s of national civil rights legislation enforcing constitutional rights of all citizens.[[435]](#cite_note-445)[[436]](#cite_note-446)

The state's conservative White voters began to support Republican presidential candidates by the mid-20th century. After this period, they supported Republicans for local and state offices as well, and most Whites became Republican Party members.[[437]](#cite_note-447) The party also attracted some minorities, but many have continued to vote for Democratic candidates. The shift to the Republican Party is much-attributed to the fact the Democratic Party became increasingly [liberal](/wiki/Liberalism_in_the_United_States "Liberalism in the United States") during the 20th century, and thus increasingly out-of-touch with the average Texas voter.[[438]](#cite_note-HistoryTXvoters-448) As Texas was always a [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") state, voters switched to the Republicans, which now more closely reflected their beliefs.[[438]](#cite_note-HistoryTXvoters-448)[[439]](#cite_note-PBSredstate-449) Commentators have also attributed the shift to Republican political consultant [Karl Rove](/wiki/Karl_Rove "Karl Rove"), who managed numerous political campaigns in Texas in the 1980s and 1990s.[[439]](#cite_note-PBSredstate-449) Other stated reasons included court-ordered redistricting and the growing Sun Belt cities' conservatism unlike the liberal tradition of their northeastern counterparts as well as the popularity of Republican party in the suburbs.[[121]](#cite_note-Calvert-2017-126)

The [2003 Texas redistricting](/wiki/2003_Texas_redistricting "2003 Texas redistricting") of Congressional districts led by Republican [Tom DeLay](/wiki/Tom_DeLay "Tom DeLay"), was called by *The New York Times* "an extreme case of partisan [gerrymandering](/wiki/Gerrymandering "Gerrymandering")".[[440]](#cite_note-NYTGerry-450) A group of Democratic legislators, the "Texas Eleven", fled the state in a [quorum](/wiki/Quorum "Quorum")-busting effort to prevent the legislature from acting, but was unsuccessful.[[441]](#cite_note-451) The state had already redistricted following the 2000 census. Despite these efforts, the legislature passed a map heavily in favor of Republicans, based on 2000 data and ignoring the estimated nearly one million new residents in the state since then. Career attorneys and analysts at the Department of Justice objected to the plan as diluting the votes of African American and Hispanic voters, but political appointees overrode them and approved it.[[440]](#cite_note-NYTGerry-450) Legal challenges to the redistricting reached the national [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") in the case *[League of United Latin American Citizens v. Perry](/wiki/League_of_United_Latin_American_Citizens_v._Perry "League of United Latin American Citizens v. Perry")* (2006), but the court ruled in favor of the state (and Republicans).[[442]](#cite_note-452)

In the [2014 Texas elections](/wiki/Texas_elections,_2014 "Texas elections, 2014"), the [Tea Party movement](/wiki/Tea_Party_movement "Tea Party movement") made large gains, with numerous Tea Party favorites being elected into office, including [Dan Patrick](/wiki/Dan_Patrick_(politician) "Dan Patrick (politician)") as lieutenant governor,[[443]](#cite_note-Fernandez-453)[[444]](#cite_note-454) [Ken Paxton](/wiki/Ken_Paxton "Ken Paxton") as attorney general,[[443]](#cite_note-Fernandez-453)[[445]](#cite_note-Grissom-455) in addition to numerous other candidates[[445]](#cite_note-Grissom-455) including conservative Republican [Greg Abbott](/wiki/Greg_Abbott "Greg Abbott") as governor.[[446]](#cite_note-456)

Texas voters lean toward [fiscal conservatism](/wiki/Fiscal_conservatism "Fiscal conservatism"), while enjoying the benefits of significant federal investment in the state in military and other facilities achieved by the power of the Solid South in the 20th century. They also tend to have [socially conservative](/wiki/Social_conservatism "Social conservatism") values.[[260]](#cite_note-TPoliticalCulture1-268)[[447]](#cite_note-457)

Since 1980, most Texas voters have supported Republican presidential candidates. Austin, Dallas, Houston, San Antonio, and El Paso consistently lean Democratic in both local and statewide elections.[[123]](#cite_note-City_Journal-2016-128) Residents of counties along the [Rio Grande](/wiki/Rio_Grande "Rio Grande") closer to the [Mexico–United States border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border"), where there are many Latino residents, generally vote for Democratic Party candidates, while most other rural and suburban areas of Texas have shifted to voting for Republican Party candidates.[[448]](#cite_note-458)[[449]](#cite_note-459)

As of the [midterm elections of 2022](/wiki/2024_United_States_elections "2024 United States elections"), a large majority of the members of Texas's [U.S. House](/wiki/U.S._House_of_Representatives "U.S. House of Representatives") delegation are [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), along with both [U.S. Senators](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"). In the [119th United States Congress](/wiki/119th_United_States_Congress "119th United States Congress"), of the 38 [congressional districts in Texas](/wiki/Texas%27s_congressional_districts "Texas's congressional districts"), 25 are held by Republicans and 13 by Democrats. Texas's Senators are [John Cornyn](/wiki/John_Cornyn "John Cornyn") and [Ted Cruz](/wiki/Ted_Cruz "Ted Cruz"). Since 1994, Texans have not elected a Democrat to a statewide office. The state's Democratic voters are made up primarily by liberal and [minority groups](/wiki/Minority_groups "Minority groups") in Austin, Beaumont, Dallas, El Paso, Houston, and San Antonio as well as minority voters in East and South Texas.[[450]](#cite_note-460)[[451]](#cite_note-461)[[452]](#cite_note-462)
According to a study by the [Cato Institute](/wiki/Cato_Institute "Cato Institute"), Texas ranks last in personal freedom among the states, by factors including incarceration rates, cannabis laws, civil asset forfeiture policies, educational freedom, gambling laws, marriage freedom, and travel freedom.[[453]](#cite_note-463)[[454]](#cite_note-464)

Lesser parties that have ballot access are the [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Texas "Green Party of Texas") and the [Libertarian Party](/wiki/Libertarian_Party_(United_States) "Libertarian Party (United States)").[[455]](#cite_note-465)

### Criminal law

Main article: [Crime in Texas](/wiki/Crime_in_Texas "Crime in Texas")

See also: [List of law enforcement agencies in Texas](/wiki/List_of_law_enforcement_agencies_in_Texas "List of law enforcement agencies in Texas")

Texas has a reputation of very harsh criminal punishment for criminal offenses. It is one of the 32 states that practice [capital punishment](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_Texas "Capital punishment in Texas"), and since the [US Supreme Court](/wiki/US_Supreme_Court "US Supreme Court") allowed capital punishment to resume in 1976, 40% of all U.S. executions have taken place in Texas.[[456]](#cite_note-466) In 2018, Texas had the [8th highest incarceration rate in the U.S.](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_incarceration_and_correctional_supervision_rate "List of U.S. states and territories by incarceration and correctional supervision rate")[[457]](#cite_note-467) Texas also has strong [right of self-defense](/wiki/Right_of_self-defense "Right of self-defense") and [self defense](/wiki/Self-defense "Self-defense") laws, allowing citizens to use lethal force to defend themselves, their families, or their property.[[458]](#cite_note-468) Texas has one of the strictest [abortion](/wiki/Abortion_in_Texas "Abortion in Texas") bans in the country.[[459]](#cite_note-469)

["Between the Creeks"](https://texashistory.unt.edu/ark:/67531/metapth752794/m2/1/high_res_d/Between%20_the_Creeks_by_Gwen_Pettit_compiled_by_Melinda_Fisher.pdf) (PDF). *texashistory.unt.edu*. Retrieved December 28, 2020. "The Southern states, especially the hills of Tennessee and Alabama, were impoverished; war tensions still split neighborhoods. Soon, empty houses had crude signs that stated that the former inhabitants had "Gone to Texas." Church records, also, had the phrase, "Gone to Texas" by numerous names on their roles. So many families left Maury County, Tenn., to settle in eastern Collin County, just across East Fork, that several communities, such as Culleoka, have names directly from south Maury County. That group joined relatives that had come here in the 1850s. Most new immigrants had some link to Collin County, which brought them here. They stayed with relatives and friends until they could find a place to settle. Landowners recruited farmers from the old states by persuading relatives and former neighbors to come. However, numerous families, in the pioneer tradition, loaded their children and belongings in a wagon and headed toward the unknown west. Clarksville, Bonham and Dallas newspapers reported how many wagons passed through each day and how many were camped on the "jockey yards" waiting to find a place to settle. Some of these had sold farms and had money to buy land, but most of these immigrants became tenant farmers and worked on shares."

115. **[^](#cite_ref-120)** ["'War against war': Americans for peace in World War I – National Constitution Center"](https://constitutioncenter.org/blog/war-against-war-americans-for-peace-in-world-war-i). *National Constitution Center – constitutioncenter.org*. [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20201112022807/https://constitutioncenter.org/blog/war-against-war-americans-for-peace-in-world-war-i) from the original on November 12, 2020. Retrieved December 28, 2020. "Morris Hillquit sought to keep alive the ties of his Socialist Party to its comrades abroad. Senator Robert La Follette filled many a speech with praise for progressives in other countries who shared his hatred for militarism. Henry Ford chartered an ocean liner to transport himself and dozens of other activists across the Atlantic, where they lobbied neutral governments to embrace a peace plan they would press on the warring powers. These Americans, like most critics of the war elsewhere in the world, wanted to create a new global order based on cooperative relationships between nation states and their gradual disarmament. Militarism, they argued, isolated peoples behind walls of mutual fear and loathing. Until April 1917, this formidable coalition of idealists—or realists—did much to keep the nation at peace. They may even have had a majority of Americans on their side until just weeks before Congress, at Wilson's behest, voted to declare war. To prevent that from happening, peace activists pressed for a national referendum on the question, confident that "the people" would recoil from fighting and paying the bills in order to help one group of European powers conquer another."
